speaker: interviewer
text: We're gonna start in a few seconds. I still have to run a few--

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said go to the hospital--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and work there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did he say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course that hospital-- so you wouldn't forget your medicine. I mean, it was a great joke. Everybody said that I'm an idiot, but nobody asked for a diploma. So you say you are a doctor and you are, but I was not yet acclimatized for a concentration camp. I was stupid, and I-- and stayed alive, and the others did not. Now, that hospital didn't exist. We had to create it. He chose a doctor. Well, he made a very good choice. He had a doctor from Prague, which I only superficially knew, Otto Heller, who was an intern--

speaker: interviewer
text: Heller?

speaker: interviewee
text: Heller, yeah. H-E-L-L-E-R, who was a very known internist in Prague, known in the Jewish community, but known to others, too. I mean, there was no real [INAUDIBLE]. And he would be the head of it. And there came another doctor, and said I was supposed to be the head of the medical team. Us men didn't know what-- from where. He comes out of the left field, a tall, strong man. So says, so you work with-- with Otto Heller. We still talked about names, et cetera. That got lost later. And then all the time [? beware ?] that these two doctors were the good and the bad. Otto Heller was a real decent doctor who tried to help wherever he could, and the other wanted to have the advantage of getting a better position, say.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hans Hellman, of course. I'll never forget that name. That boss ended badly, but I said to him, it was a real split in good and bad. He was a-- we finally heard, you know, he was a medical doctor. He was-- he had a laboratory in Vienna, so he knew enough medical terms to bluff, but he had never treated patients, but he had them see one position that was about equal to-- to Otto, who was an extremely decent person. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] At Yom Kippur in this year, so very soon after our arrival there, I experienced the first of these bad things that were called selections. There were two selections-- selections for-- of arriving people and to work for us, and people to be executed at the entrance. And then there was the other selection, which took time-- which took place time and again selecting people that were not active enough anymore to be of any worth, not in our camp. Our camp was still 5,000 as we came in, but next door in the women's lager, frauenlager, took-- there was a selection on the evening of Yom Kippur of-- in '43. Of course, I didn't-- I had heard rumors, but had no idea what it really is. The first thing that happened that everybody has to go in other camps inside the barracks. Nobody is allowed to look out or go out of the barrack. I was very curious and went to the gate of our hospital barrack, and said, hospitals should have some privilege, and opened a little bit the gate to look out. And what I saw were naked women in the women's lager thrown-- I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thrown onto a car-- I mean, a truck. So it seemed to me younger women-- I mean, at some distance. They were all naked, and they were really thrown, and with hands up in the air, and legs are spread out there, screamed [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Screamed what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some Jewish religious--

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], I think it was, yeah. I close the gate, and then I really had for the first time seen what was going on there, and yet our camp seemed to be privileged. We were still families in very bad conditions-- poor food, terrible thirst, standing in appells. What's the English word when you go and sit? OK. Like it's a military. Yeah. Women-- all the women with children in the rain and later in snow, but you had to stand, and they had to count. And if the amount did not fit, and it didn't fit because by then already certain people had died. They always made a mistake by counting the deaths before-- dead ones before the appell. So they had to start from the beginning.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was a very hard life, but again, I was young. I could stand it and worked as a doctor, which always was crucial for me. I worked hard, and we-- our work was very difficult because we didn't have much. These appells, how long would they take?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Hard to say now. I mean, well, maybe half an hour, but it could be an hour or two, and some people would faint, break down, and it was difficult for the SS men who run and down counting it. And they got furious and made mistakes. Oh, we start from the beginning, and it was endless.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was all outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside in front of-- in-- in the mud, and depending what weather there was. It fall, you know. It then was very soon, winter. And the winter up there is very-- very heavy. It's flat, and it's not a hill. Very far in the back you see some high mountains, which I think belonged already to Czechoslovakia. There was a border there, but the area there is flat. When it's windy, you know there's it's no protection. It just blows you away. But I was young, and I had a decent work. Most people had nothing to do. I mean, a few people who were useful to the camp were used-- an electrician, or a plumber, or-- not that there was plumbing, but there was a wash room where some water was. And there were latrines that had to be-- so if you were a plumber, you at least got work there and got a little more bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still the clothes we came in, which was also an exception. Later on, they'll change that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you kept warmer than the prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and I was inside. You see, most people had to go out of their barracks to go to the latrines, the toilets, which was sometimes a walk of half an hour in the mud for all the people. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the beginning, and then, of course, the shoes got stuck in the mud, and they had only one shoe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about you? Did you have shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had shoes. Of course, I was still in the hospital. We had some advantages. First of all, when patients died, there remained a little bit bread or an extra soup, which was distributed to the personnel, or sometimes we give it to the patients, too, but we slowly got a little bit more adjusted to life in a camp and thought about ourselves. So I had still the advantage of being a doctor there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said at the beginning that there was no hospital yet when you got there.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a barrack, and they said, you make a hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you guys made the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we made the hospital. There was one sergeant-- master sergeant, we would call it in military, an SS man, an elderly man who was a medic, which was a kind of nurse, over nurse, who did take care of all the things as a big shot doctor, SS doctor, but not one to get involved with. He was the middle man between what we badly need, and he was very decent.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't know. I'm really sad that I don't know, but I knew that they had somehow saved his life. Some of our survivors saved his life after that. Otherwise, he would have been working with Mengele and his doctors. He would've been executed, and he was the person to approach and tell him-- we said, we need badly insulin. And he would tell her, insulin is not allowed to give to Jews. What the logic is, I don't know. Serum-- we had a terrible diphtheria epidemic. Serum, which is made out of the blood of people who had the illness is not allowed to give to Jews. So people died until we found some way to deal with the others. It was even medically a very interesting situation. We had no x-rays. We had to listen to the chest to find when people had pneumonia, and they did have pneumonia. It didn't help us too much, because antibiotics were not invented yet, but chemotherapy, which outside was available was not available for us, except a black market smuggled in for people who ported in with the transportation, which they are transports. They were somewhere else, and the luggage remained.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were most of the diseases that you did treat there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Diphtheria, pneumonia, menigitis-- severe ones. And then there were very bad [INAUDIBLE] infections of the legs, because the people lost their shoes, they had cuts on their feet. The cuts got infected, and some-- there  you saw things that you usually don't see. When we approached the sergeant, we said, we cannot treat this. We have no means to treat this well. He said, you should see our soldier on the Russian front. So they had found some kind of justification. Say to-- say I have to suffer on the-- so at the Russian front. Or--

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't just take these people and kill them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in our-- we were a privileged camp for six months. Now, the question was, why? Our speculation was the same thing we had heard about Theresienstadt and the Red Cross that the Red Cross had heard about Auschwitz, and they wanted to show the Red Cross, look, the whole families live here, can get together. That was the only explanation we could assume. I have never found out what the reason was. After six months, everybody-- everybody was supposed to be transported another camp. Detours came. Birkenau is too severe, too difficult, run down. We have a better place where we will deport you. And I marched six-- or six and seven-- it took two days of next year, '44. All these 6,000 people I had come with to Auschwitz were taken out of the camp in a place which was called the quarantine, waiting for the train to be transported to the next camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you one of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was one of them. The name of that fictitious camp-- I can't remember. It's in my book. Not important.

speaker: interviewer
text: As you talk about this, I-- I just want to-- I see here on the table of contents of your book that you have number 13, which is called 'Mengele, Bengele, the Hospital and Joseph Mengele.' Does that-- does that come just now?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little later.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it really comes earlier. You are right. It comes earlier because he-- he was more instrumental to what happened there. What happened was that one patient who was severely depressed and had refused to eat in his barrack was brought to us in the hospital, and we really though we couldn't really save him. We had no IVs. We had no way to feed him, so one of us was sitting with him and spoon by spoon feeding him, and suddenly he got out of the depression and became manic. He was a bipolar manic depressive psychotic-- and became very cheerful, started to eat, and started to sing. And the next day when-- by the Mengele was the chief doctor who came a couple of times a week to look after the hospital. And we knew he was coming, and of course everything had to be clean and quiet.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got very worried. How would this manic person who is screaming, and yelling, and singing behave? So we put a nurse on his side who tried to keep in-- in his bed. And Mengele came and made his visit, and he started to scream. And said, Mengele is coming. Be quiet. So he started to sing, Mengele, bengele. Bengele means scandal. Mengele, bengele, bengele. And Mengele took out his revolver and wanted to shoot him at the place. And Leo Heller, the chief doctor, made the gesture, as if he wanted to calm the patient, but really calming Mengele. He put back his revolver, his gun, and said to the sergeant, over. An hour later came an ambulance, and this man was picked up. Now, we ask, where-- where did you take him? And there was just a kind of gesture, which we knew. What did it mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was took-- taken back to the crematoria, shot, and-- I mean, for psychotics, we had heard before that that was their solution for psychotics, even psychotic Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is [HEBREW] mean? Why was there a chapter called [HEBREW] in here?

speaker: interviewee
text: [HEBREW], which is Hebrew, means the hope, and became later the national anthem of Israel. So I was saying after six months that whole group of people, me included, were in this quarantine barracks waiting for the train. It was supposed to take us to the new camp, and it didn't come, and it didn't come, and we got in formation. There's no train on the ramp. We got underground information it's very bad. Be prepared. And we knew that--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think? What do you really know?

speaker: interviewee
text: By then, we really had heard that we would go to the gas chambers. Our six months of-- of what they're called, quarantine, waiting for the Red Cross, were over, and we would just be all totally executed, because by then we knew what was going on there. I fall-- fell asleep on the second night. Day and night we had already waited there when I heard somebody calling my name, and somebody called me out and a few other people all from the hospital where I work, and said you have to go back to the hospital. And I was saved. The next day the whole 6,000-- 5,000-- it wasn't 5,000 anymore because some had died by then-- about 4,600 went into the gas chambers. And while they were in the gas chambers, before the doors were closed, they are singing the [HEBREW] and the Czech national anthem. And the chief of the whole Auschwitz camp and Mengele were standing there and laughing, and then they closed the door. That was described by people who were there. Well, mercy help us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not there at that moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, by then I was back in the hospital. It was unusual, because usually that hospitals are the ones that go first. We never quite understood, but the-- our assumption and some of the interpretation we heard from people who-- who knew more than we knew said it was a fight between Mengele and-- and the head of the whole camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew it, of course. But I-- he had the right to say I have the commander from Berlin to liquidate the camp [INAUDIBLE], and Mengele was saying, but not my hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, he was fond of the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He wasn't fond of it, but it was a matter of-- of power. That-- I cannot swear that that is the explanation. He was-- he had some interest in the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know him well? How well did you know him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know him as well as one of the people standing there when he comes. He had never anything to do with me, or I had never anything to do with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your professional opinion about the man?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was cold like ice, and he assumed-- I am sure he assumed what he was doing was in the interest of the movement of creating a big, German, powerful, imperial, with no foreign blood, which is a strange concept-- would find any rights or [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him take any pleasure in any of the things he did?

speaker: interviewee
text: I say always that he was totally detached. I mean, when he puts this psychotic, the manic patient, who was a lovely young man, send him to be executed, that was just like you sent another patient for surgery. There was no particular-- I mean, I have never seen him [INAUDIBLE] with newcomers. But I have seen pictures, and I know and have listened to people who where there. I-- he did it as a duty. This is no excuse, of course, but of course he was also very clever. He knew how to save himself. I'm sure he had a fortune of-- of gold and money stolen from the people who arrived in Auschwitz, sent to Switzerland or wherever, and when he went to South America-- but I mean this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- did you see any Hungarian Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or any gypsies there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, first of all, there was a gypsy camp, but that-- I got in touch with this a little later. In summer of '44, we are still in our hospital in the Czech family camp. No, cannot be. Let me think. The-- the disaster was in March, so it must have started before-- came the mass transportations from Hungary. The Hungarian Jews somehow survived until the last year. I thought it was '44, but it somehow doesn't figure out. No, it was '44 because I was back to the hospital, of course. It was '44. And there were selected-- only the younger people were put into a camp, and the others went straight into the gas chambers-- 10 thousands of people still in relatively good shape because they had to eat in Hungary not everything, but enough to survive and-- and had still a more or less normal life until the Germans took over and just started these transportations. There's a camp next to us which at the time was empty prior to the arrival of the Hungarian Jews, which was made into a camp of young women, which were then supposed to go out to work. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And these young women had lost everything. They were shorn. They had no hair. They had nothing of their original clothes. They got little summer dresses that they had collected from others. They're freezing. The nights were still very cold, and were totally confused. They didn't know what's happened. They had no warning, no understanding. We, at least, had rumors which we could put together. Some of them were very young. They came to-- there was an electrically charged fence between the two camps-- our hospital camp and that Hungarian camp. They came as close as they could come, and there were some difficulties in communications. There were a few that talked a little bit Slovak, and we usually talked Czech so we could communicate, but more or less we communicate by movement, gesture. There was a one-- I take out from many little tragedies I saw that is girls, where a small number of survivors from a big transport that was selected-- the chimneys were smoking all the time, and this is-- it was a hectic time, a terrible time. They didn't understand it. They didn't want to believe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, one of these girls spoke a little German and explained to me that while she was sent to the washroom, which really was a washroom, the other washrooms were gas chambers-- was raped by one of the SS people. A really beautiful-- even shorn and desperate-- young woman, and she thought that this was the worst thing. She had never had any contact with a man, and that was the worst thing that can happen to her. So she was in pain. So I had some aspirin in there. We throw it over the-- some aspirin over the fence, some aspirin, and talked to her, and she says I wonder if he comes again? I mean, the greatest fear like in rape traumatized young woman is, what if it happens again? So I told her what to do. I said you just make yourself as tall as you can be and don't look at him, or she wouldn't even know that it was he or any of the-- just look into the emptiness. Ignore, but don't face. Don't confront. You look like any one looks. There's no reason why you should be more in danger than anybody else.

words.text: that this was the worst thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I lost contact. She went with one of the work transports out of the camp. And there were a few other-- other things. I also was in a little romantic contact with a woman who-- who was sitting and talked pretty well German, and I explained a little bit what was going on, and she was little old, maybe in her later 20s. I was just a teenager, and had understanding for the situation, and had a kind of philosophical outlook. There was one flower, I think, which I took out and showed her, so we had this kind of funny thing going, over the fence, and--
